(Oxford: Oxford U. Press, 1999), 140. For a vigorous statement of this position, see Eugene Volokh,
Freedom of Speech and Information Privacy: The Troubling Implications of a Right to Stop People from
Speaking About You, 52 Stan.L Rev 1049 (2000).

1ère chambre, section B, Arret du 20 Septembre 2001. In that case, the court allowed the publication of a

62
         Many more examples can be offered—most especially involving nudity, and most

especially involving the internet. In German and French legal culture, we still find much

the attitude that we found in the 1877 decision regarding Ingres’ nude sketch of Mme.

Moitessier: One ought to have control over one’s nude image. This means, of course,

that one can sell the rights to image, just as one can in America194 —at least provisionally.

Nevertheless, there are limits. In one 1974 case, for example, a French actress was

permitted to supress movie scenes in which she had willingly appeared naked: One’s

nude image is simply not definitively alienable under continental norms. 195 In other

cases, models have been able to suppress the republication of their nude photos in

magazines other than the ones they posed for—a matter in which “French law,” a

commentator observes approvingly, “is . . . totally opposed to American law.”196 The

same sort of attitude has also affected both continental cyberlaw and the continental law

of public nudity. The internet has produced two recent leading cases in particular, one in

Germany and one in France, in both of which courts imposed liability on internet service

providers which housed nude images of celebrities. In the German decision, Steffi Graf,

the former tennis star, successfully sued Microsoft for its refusal to guarantee that it
